learning_objectives: Identify and classify gastric lymph node stations 1–16 per JGCA 5th edition
Distinguish D1, D1+, and D2 lymphadenectomy and justify D2 as the standard for curative surgery
Describe the operative steps of total gastrectomy and distal gastrectomy with Roux-en-Y reconstruction
Apply FLOT perioperative chemotherapy regimen and interpret its evidence base
Recognise and manage post-gastrectomy syndromes (dumping, bile reflux, B12 deficiency)
Classify GEJ tumours using the Siewert system and select the appropriate operative approach
Stage gastric cancer with TNM 8th edition and interpret pTNM categories for adjuvant treatment decisions
Identify CDH1 mutation implications and recommend prophylactic gastrectomy appropriately

sections.title: Surgical Anatomy and Lymph Node Stations

blocks.content: The stomach is supplied by five named arteries arising from the coeliac axis: left gastric (dominant supply to lesser curvature), right gastric (lesser curvature from hepatic artery), left gastroepiploic (greater curvature from splenic artery), right gastroepiploic (greater curvature from gastroduodenal artery), and short gastric arteries (fundus from splenic artery). The corresponding lymph node stations are numbered 1–16 per the JGCA 5th edition, forming the anatomical basis for the D-level dissection classification. Understanding this arterial supply is essential for both safe dissection and oncologically adequate lymphadenectomy.

blocks.title: JGCA Lymph Node Stations — Anatomical Groups and Dissection Levels
rows: Short gastric, left and right gastroepiploic
At origin of left gastric artery
Along hepatic artery in hepatoduodenal ligament

blocks.caption: Figure 1. Gastric lymph node stations 1–12 (JGCA 5th edition). D1 dissection removes perigastric stations 1–6. D2 dissection additionally removes stations 7–12 along named vascular structures.
blocks.alt: Stomach anterior view with numbered lymph node stations 1-12

blocks.title: Board Pearl — D2 Standard
blocks.content: D2 lymphadenectomy is the standard for curative-intent gastrectomy in all guideline frameworks (NCCN, ESMO, JGCA). The 15-year follow-up of the Dutch D1D2 trial confirmed a survival advantage for D2 over D1 (29% vs 21% cancer-specific survival, p=0.034). A minimum of **16 lymph nodes** must be examined for adequate pathological staging (JGCA); AJCC requires ≥16 for valid N-staging.

blocks.title: Station 16 Para-aortic Nodes — Critical Distinction
blocks.content: Station 16 (para-aortic nodes) is NOT part of D2 dissection — positive para-aortic nodes represent **M1 disease** in the TNM 8th edition classification. The JCOG9501 trial showed that D3 dissection (including para-aortic nodes) conferred no survival benefit over D2 in gastric cancer and significantly increased morbidity. If station 16 nodes are found positive at staging laparoscopy or intraoperative frozen section, curative gastrectomy should be aborted.

blocks.title: FLOT4 Trial (Al-Batran, Lancet 2019) — Still Standard in 2025
blocks.content: **Population:** 716 patients with resectable gastric or GEJ adenocarcinoma, perioperative FLOT (docetaxel/oxaliplatin/leucovorin/5-FU) vs ECF/ECX. **Result:** Median OS 50 vs 35 months (HR 0.77, p=0.012); pCR 16% vs 6%. **Practice change:** FLOT remains the Category 1 Western standard perioperative regimen per NCCN 2025 and ESMO 2024. **2025 update — emerging option**: Nivolumab + FLOT (DANTE/MATTERHORN trials, 2024) shows improved pCR in HER2-negative, PD-L1 CPS ≥5 patients and is listed as Category 2A in NCCN 2025 for selected patients. Not yet universal standard but rapidly evolving.

**Distal gastrectomy** (60–75% gastrectomy) is indicated for antral and body tumours when a 5cm proximal margin from the tumour can be achieved. Reconstruction options: Billroth II (gastrojejunostomy), Roux-en-Y gastrojejunostomy (preferred to prevent bile reflux gastritis). **Total gastrectomy** is required for fundic and diffuse-type tumours (signet ring/linitis plastica) and when adequate proximal margin cannot be achieved with distal gastrectomy. Reconstruction is by oesophagojejunostomy (Roux-en-Y). D2 dissection is performed with both procedures.

blocks.title: Distal vs Total Gastrectomy — Indications and Key Operative Considerations
rows: Fundic, diffuse-type, or when 5cm proximal margin not achievable
Standard (resect only if station 10 direct infiltration)
Complete absence of gastric function; B12 deficiency universal

blocks.title: Common Pitfall — Proximal Margin
blocks.content: For diffuse-type (signet ring) gastric cancer, a **5cm proximal margin** is required due to submucosal spread. For intestinal-type gastric cancer, 3cm is accepted. Intraoperative frozen section of the proximal margin is mandatory if doubt exists. Positive proximal margin is R1 regardless of the distance from the gross tumour.

blocks.title: Minimally Invasive Gastrectomy — Current Evidence
blocks.content: Laparoscopic distal gastrectomy (LDG) is now standard for early gastric cancer at high-volume Asian centres, with equivalent oncological outcomes and reduced short-term morbidity compared to open surgery (KLASS-01 trial: 5-year OS 94.2% LDG vs 93.3% open). For advanced gastric cancer, laparoscopic total gastrectomy (KLASS-02) shows non-inferior short-term surgical outcomes. Robotic gastrectomy has increasing adoption but lacks definitive survival data over laparoscopic approaches.

Post-gastrectomy syndromes are functional and nutritional sequelae of altered gastric anatomy. They are more frequent after total than after distal gastrectomy and more common after Billroth II than Roux-en-Y reconstruction. Dumping syndrome (early and late) is the most common functional complication, affecting up to 20% of patients after total gastrectomy. Nutritional deficiencies — particularly vitamin B12, iron, and calcium — require lifelong monitoring and supplementation. Understanding the timing and mechanism of each syndrome is essential for accurate diagnosis and targeted management.

blocks.title: Board Pearl
blocks.content: After total gastrectomy, **vitamin B12 must be supplemented lifelong** by intramuscular injection (hydroxocobalamin 1mg IM every 3 months) — there is no intrinsic factor and oral supplementation is unreliable. Failure to supplement causes irreversible subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord.

blocks.title: Annual Nutritional Surveillance After Gastrectomy
blocks.content: Annual blood panel is mandatory for all post-gastrectomy patients: **FBC** (iron deficiency, megaloblastic anaemia), **serum B12 and folate**, **ferritin and transferrin saturation**, **calcium, phosphate, and 25-OH vitamin D**, **albumin** (nutritional marker). DEXA scan at 2 years to assess for post-gastrectomy osteoporosis (reduced calcium absorption). Register patients with a dietitian at discharge — structured nutritional counselling reduces post-gastrectomy malnutrition.

Accurate staging of gastric cancer is the cornerstone of treatment planning. The AJCC/UICC TNM 8th edition (2017) provides the current staging framework. Clinical staging (cTNM) is based on CT thorax/abdomen/pelvis, EUS for T and N staging, and staging laparoscopy for cT3-T4 or node-positive disease. Pathological staging (pTNM) requires a minimum of 16 lymph nodes examined for adequate N-staging. The distinction between N1 (1–2 positive nodes), N2 (3–6), and N3 (≥7: N3a = 7–15, N3b = ≥16) is critical for adjuvant therapy decisions and prognosis.

blocks.title: TNM 8th Edition Gastric Cancer Staging — Key Categories
rows: Tumour confined to mucosa (lamina propria/muscularis mucosae)
ESD eligible if differentiated, no LVI
ESD if SM1 ≤500μm, differentiated, no LVI; otherwise gastrectomy + D1+
Gastrectomy + D2; perioperative chemotherapy if ≥T2 in Western practice
Tumour penetrates subserosa (without peritoneal or adjacent organ invasion)
Perioperative FLOT standard; staging laparoscopy mandatory
High peritoneal recurrence risk; staging laparoscopy mandatory
Tumour invades adjacent structures (aorta, liver, pancreas, colon)
Extended resection if R0 achievable; neoadjuvant chemotherapy recommended
Stage IIIC — very high recurrence risk; all adjuvant therapy
Distant metastases (including station 16, peritoneal cytology +, peritoneal implants)

blocks.title: Staging Laparoscopy — When It Is Mandatory
blocks.content: Staging laparoscopy with peritoneal washings (cytology) is recommended by NCCN for all cT3-T4 or node-positive gastric cancers before laparotomy. It detects occult peritoneal disease in **20–30%** of clinically resectable cases — preventing futile major surgery. Positive peritoneal cytology alone (P0 CY1) upgrades disease to M1 in JGCA staging (though AJCC 8th does not classify CY1 as M1 if no macroscopic peritoneal implants — know your guideline framework).

blocks.name: Dutch D1D2 Trial — 15-Year Follow-Up
blocks.citation: Songun I et al. Lancet Oncology 2010; 11(5):439–449
blocks.population: 711 patients with resectable gastric cancer; randomised to D1 vs D2 lymphadenectomy
blocks.intervention: D1 (perigastric nodes) vs D2 (D1 + named vascular nodes including stations 7–11) lymphadenectomy
blocks.result: 15-year cancer-specific survival: 29% D2 vs 21% D1 (p=0.034). D2 associated with lower locoregional recurrence (12% vs 22%). Initial higher operative mortality for D2 (10% vs 4%) driven by non-specialist centres; in specialist centres, morbidity was equivalent.
blocks.practice_change: D2 is the established standard for curative gastrectomy in all guidelines. Long-term follow-up confirms survival benefit previously obscured by early operative mortality. Experienced surgeons at volume centres achieve D2 safely.

blocks.title: Minimum Lymph Node Requirements for Adequate Staging
blocks.content: **AJCC TNM 8th edition:** ≥16 lymph nodes examined required for valid N-staging. **JGCA 5th edition:** ≥16 nodes required; recommends ≥25 nodes at expert centres. If fewer than 16 nodes are examined, pN0 is classified as pNx (inadequate staging) — this affects both prognosis and adjuvant therapy decisions. Low lymph node yield is a quality marker for gastrectomy and should be audited.

Perioperative chemotherapy is the standard of care for resectable gastric and gastroesophageal junction (GEJ) adenocarcinoma in Western practice. The FLOT4 trial (Al-Batran, Lancet 2019) established FLOT (docetaxel 50mg/m², oxaliplatin 85mg/m², leucovorin 200mg/m², 5-FU 2600mg/m² continuous infusion) given every 2 weeks as the preferred perioperative regimen, replacing the previously used ECF/ECX based on the MAGIC trial (Cunningham, NEJM 2006). The regimen consists of 4 pre-operative cycles followed by 4 post-operative cycles, with surgery performed 4–6 weeks after the last pre-operative cycle.

blocks.title: FLOT Regimen — Practical Considerations
blocks.content: Key practical points for FLOT management: **(1) Toxicity profile:** Neutropenia (G3/4 in 51%), peripheral neuropathy (oxaliplatin), fatigue, nausea. G-CSF prophylaxis recommended. **(2) Response assessment:** CT restaging after 4 pre-operative cycles — if progressive disease, reassess operability. pCR or major pathological response is a favourable prognostic marker. **(3) Post-operative completion:** 4 post-operative cycles should be completed as planned; delays due to surgical complications require MDT review. **(4) Nutritional monitoring:** Weight and nutritional status must be tracked throughout — malnutrition delays surgery and reduces chemotherapy tolerance.

blocks.name: FLOT4 — Perioperative FLOT vs ECF/ECX
blocks.population: 716 patients with resectable gastric or GEJ adenocarcinoma (T2-T4 or node-positive); multicentre German phase III RCT
blocks.intervention: Perioperative FLOT (4 cycles pre-op + 4 cycles post-op) vs ECF/ECX (3 cycles pre-op + 3 cycles post-op)
blocks.result: Median OS 50 months (FLOT) vs 35 months (ECF/ECX), HR 0.77, p=0.012. Pathological complete response 16% vs 6%. 5-year OS 45% vs 36%. R0 resection rate similar (85% vs 78%). Increased toxicity with FLOT (neutropenia, diarrhoea) but manageable.
blocks.practice_change: FLOT replaced ECF/ECX as the Western standard perioperative regimen for resectable gastric and GEJ cancer. Represents a 15-month median OS improvement and 10% absolute increase in pCR rate.

blocks.title: Mandatory Biomarker Panel — NCCN 2025 / ESMO 2024
blocks.content: A comprehensive biomarker panel must be performed on ALL newly diagnosed gastric/GEJ adenocarcinomas: **(1) HER2** (IHC ± ISH): HER2+ (IHC 3+ or 2+/ISH+) — first-line trastuzumab + pembrolizumab + chemotherapy (KEYNOTE-811; Category 1, NCCN 2025/ESMO 2024). **(2) PD-L1 CPS** (combined positive score): CPS ≥5 → nivolumab + FOLFOX first-line (CheckMate 649; Category 1). **(3) MSI/MMR**: dMMR patients benefit from immunotherapy; consider omitting perioperative chemotherapy. **(4) CLDN18.2** (claudin 18 isoform 2): CLDN18.2+ (IHC ≥2+ in ≥75% tumour cells), RAS-WT, HER2-negative → zolbetuximab + FOLFOX/XELOX first-line (SPOTLIGHT/GLOW trials; FDA approved May 2024). **(5) FGFR2b**: emerging biomarker for bemarituzumab eligibility. HER2 expression may be lost at progression — **retesting at recurrence is mandatory**.

Post-gastrectomy surgical complications are classified by the Clavien-Dindo system and, for anastomotic leaks, by the International Study Group of Gastric Cancer (ISGC) leak grading (Grades A–C based on clinical impact and intervention required). The most feared early complications are anastomotic leak (especially oesophagojejunostomy after total gastrectomy) and duodenal stump leak after distal gastrectomy. Pancreatic fistula and chylous ascites are important delayed complications following D2 lymphadenectomy involving the pancreatic region and thoracic duct territory.
